extra.reasoning: This is a state machine. The property requires that in every reachable state, opted_out=true implies sent=false. The transition [t_resume_glitch] (on send_attempt if opted_out and ticks>=4: sent := true) sets sent to true while opted_out remains true, directly breaking the invariant. I must show that state is reachable. Starting from opted_out=false, sent=false, ticks=0: (1) optout -> t_optout fires: opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=0. (2-5) four tick events -> t_tick increments ticks each time (capped at 4) and keeps sent=false: after the 4th tick, ticks=4, opted_out=true, sent=false. (6) send_attempt: now opted_out=true and ticks=4>=4, so t_resume_glitch's guard holds and it fires, setting sent:=true. The resulting state has opted_out=true AND sent=true, violating globally(opted_out implies not sent). Note that on this send_attempt, t_send_blocked (guard: opted_out) is also enabled, but the spec gives no priority ordering, so t_resume_glitch is a legal transition to take; since the property must hold on EVERY reachable execution, the existence of this one execution falsifies it. The counterexample uses 6 events, within the 8-event bound.
